title: Christian ska

infobox.value: * [Contemporary Christian music](/wiki/Contemporary_Christian_music "Contemporary Christian music") * [Christian alternative rock](/wiki/Christian_alternative_rock "Christian alternative rock") * [ska](/wiki/Ska "Ska") * [ska punk](/wiki/Ska_punk "Ska punk")
Late 1970s, United States and United Kingdom
* Electric guitar * electric bass * trumpet * [tenor trombone](/wiki/Tenor_trombone "Tenor trombone") * [tenor saxophone](/wiki/Tenor_saxophone "Tenor saxophone") * drums

is a form of [Christian alternative rock](/wiki/Christian_alternative_rock "Christian alternative rock"), and subgenre of [ska](/wiki/Ska "Ska") and [ska punk](/wiki/Ska_punk "Ska punk") which is lyrically oriented toward [contemporary Christian music](/wiki/Contemporary_Christian_music "Contemporary Christian music").[[1]](#cite_note-CCM_20_1_CS-1) Though ska did not constitute a genre within the [Christian music industry](/wiki/Christian_music_industry "Christian music industry") until after [third wave ska](/wiki/Third_wave_ska "Third wave ska") had peaked in the general market,[[2]](#cite_note-Guila_6_50-2)[[3]](#cite_note-CCM_19_7-3) Christian ska continued to thrive independently into the early 2000s.[[4]](#cite_note-4)

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Music genre since the 1990s

| Christian ska | |
| --- | --- |
| Stylistic origins | * [Contemporary Christian music](/wiki/Contemporary_Christian_music "Contemporary Christian music") * [Christian alternative rock](/wiki/Christian_alternative_rock "Christian alternative rock") * [ska](/wiki/Ska "Ska") * [ska punk](/wiki/Ska_punk "Ska punk") |
| Cultural origins | Late 1970s, United States and United Kingdom |
| Typical instruments | * Electric guitar * electric bass * trumpet * [tenor trombone](/wiki/Tenor_trombone "Tenor trombone") * [tenor saxophone](/wiki/Tenor_saxophone "Tenor saxophone") * drums |
| Other topics | |
| [List of bands](/wiki/List_of_Christian_ska_bands "List of Christian ska bands") | |

**Christian ska** is a form of [Christian alternative rock](/wiki/Christian_alternative_rock "Christian alternative rock"), and subgenre of [ska](/wiki/Ska "Ska") and [ska punk](/wiki/Ska_punk "Ska punk") which is lyrically oriented toward [contemporary Christian music](/wiki/Contemporary_Christian_music "Contemporary Christian music").[[1]](#cite_note-CCM_20_1_CS-1) Though ska did not constitute a genre within the [Christian music industry](/wiki/Christian_music_industry "Christian music industry") until after [third wave ska](/wiki/Third_wave_ska "Third wave ska") had peaked in the general market,[[2]](#cite_note-Guila_6_50-2)[[3]](#cite_note-CCM_19_7-3) Christian ska continued to thrive independently into the early 2000s.[[4]](#cite_note-4)

Ska music came to be seen as "an excellent vehicle for [exhortation and praise](/wiki/Praise_and_worship_music "Praise and worship music") due to its up front lyrical style"[[5]](#cite_note-CMT_03_98-5) and upbeat, energetic, joyful sound.[[6]](#cite_note-CSTONE_26_112-6)[[7]](#cite_note-TTN_W_1998-7) While there were many smaller bands, ska in the Christian marketplace in the late 1990s came to be primarily represented by three bands: [The Insyderz](/wiki/The_Insyderz "The Insyderz"), [The O. C. Supertones](/wiki/The_O._C._Supertones "The O. C. Supertones"), and [Five Iron Frenzy](/wiki/Five_Iron_Frenzy "Five Iron Frenzy"); all of whom were commercially successful and ministry-oriented in their own right.[[8]](#cite_note-Powell_Fe_ins-8) As with third wave ska in the general market the sound was often intermingled with that of [punk](/wiki/Punk_music "Punk music"), [swing](/wiki/Swing_music "Swing music"), or [rockabilly](/wiki/Rockabilly "Rockabilly"). In parallel with mainstream ska,[[2]](#cite_note-Guila_6_50-2) many underground Christian ska bands released one or two ska based albums before completely genre-hopping away from ska or dissolving.[[9]](#cite_note-JJT_RBW_2000-9) Even the sounds of the "big three" tended to evolve. By 2002, one critic described the horn section of the Supertones as "providing color instead of the dominant sound".[[10]](#cite_note-CCM_25_4-10)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Christian_ska&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

### Early years (late 1970s through 1980s)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Christian_ska&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Early years (late 1970s through 1980s)")]

While Christian bands have played ska since the days of [Jesus music](/wiki/Jesus_music "Jesus music"),[[11]](#cite_note-Sabatino_1999-11) one of the first well documented Christian bands to play ska was Ishmael United, which operated in the late 1970s and early 1980s from the United Kingdom.[[12]](#cite_note-Powell_Fe_ishm-12)[[13]](#cite_note-CSTONE_25_109-13) Their sound was [new wave](/wiki/New_wave_music "New wave music")[[14]](#cite_note-CCM_2_11-14) or [two tone](/wiki/2_Tone_(music_genre) "2 Tone (music genre)"), similar to other British bands such as [The Specials](/wiki/The_Specials "The Specials") or [Madness](/wiki/Madness_(band) "Madness (band)"). Another early Christian ska band was The Israelites, formed in 1989.[[7]](#cite_note-TTN_W_1998-7) They played a traditional style of ska[[15]](#cite_note-HM_72-15) similar to [the Skatalites](/wiki/The_Skatalites "The Skatalites") or [Desmond Dekker](/wiki/Desmond_Dekker "Desmond Dekker"). They released two albums before Christian ska matured.[[9]](#cite_note-JJT_RBW_2000-9)

### Mainstream impact (late 1990s through mid 2000s)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Christian_ska&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Mainstream impact (late 1990s through mid 2000s)")]

Whereas in mainstream markets the popularity of ska had peaked about 1996,[[2]](#cite_note-Guila_6_50-2) the Christian music marketplace is known for being significantly behind trends in the general market.[[1]](#cite_note-CCM_20_1_CS-1)[[16]](#cite_note-Powell_Fe_dct-16)[[17]](#cite_note-YW_XV2-17) Critics usually cite The O.C. Supertones as having been the first Christian ska band to break into the national spotlight.[[3]](#cite_note-CCM_19_7-3)[[7]](#cite_note-TTN_W_1998-7)[[9]](#cite_note-JJT_RBW_2000-9)[[18]](#cite_note-Powell_Fe_sup-18)[[19]](#cite_note-CSTONE_26_111-19) The 1996 [Cornerstone Festival](/wiki/Cornerstone_Festival "Cornerstone Festival") was pivotal for two of the bands which would come to represent Christian ska. Both [Five Iron Frenzy](/wiki/Five_Iron_Frenzy "Five Iron Frenzy")[[20]](#cite_note-CSTONE_32_124-20) and [The Insyderz](/wiki/The_Insyderz "The Insyderz") played and got signed as a result, though only the latter played on an official stage.[[8]](#cite_note-Powell_Fe_ins-8)[[21]](#cite_note-Powell_Fe_fif-21)

By early 1997, ska had achieved a large enough following within Christian music to get noticed by the mainstream Christian music media. The January issue of *CCM* stated that "the Christian ska revolution is in full motion",[[3]](#cite_note-CCM_19_7-3) and that Summer the magazine spotlighted four ska bands.[[22]](#cite_note-CCM_20_1_CS_2-22) These were [Squad 5-O](/wiki/Squad_5-O "Squad 5-O") (from [Savannah, Georgia](/wiki/Savannah,_Georgia "Savannah, Georgia")), [Five Iron Frenzy](/wiki/Five_Iron_Frenzy "Five Iron Frenzy") (from [Denver, Colorado](/wiki/Denver,_Colorado "Denver, Colorado")), [The Insyderz](/wiki/The_Insyderz "The Insyderz") (from [Detroit](/wiki/Detroit "Detroit"), [Michigan](/wiki/Michigan "Michigan")), and the [OC Supertones](/wiki/OC_Supertones "OC Supertones") (from [Orange County, California](/wiki/Orange_County,_California "Orange County, California")). Of these Squad 5-O was unique in that they were [hornless](/wiki/Ska_punk "Ska punk"), their sound would later completely move away from any form of ska, and they would eventually sign to a [general market label](/wiki/Capitol_Records "Capitol Records"). The remaining three bands, sometimes known as the "big three",[[8]](#cite_note-Powell_Fe_ins-8) would come to dominate the ska sound in Christian markets. Each of these bands was founded and signed to record labels in or before 1996,[[23]](#cite_note-CCM_19_6-23) was commercially successful within Christian music,[[24]](#cite_note-HM_01_92-24)[[25]](#cite_note-CCM_3_4-25) and outlasted their contemporaries by several years.

Each of the big three were generally characterized as having unique orientations toward their audiences. [The Supertones](/wiki/The_Supertones "The Supertones") were known for being blatantly [evangelical](/wiki/Evangelism "Evangelism")[[18]](#cite_note-Powell_Fe_sup-18)[[25]](#cite_note-CCM_3_4-25) and included segments of preaching in each performance.[[26]](#cite_note-CCM_23_12-26) They also had the honor of opening for [Pope John Paul II](/wiki/Pope_John_Paul_II "Pope John Paul II") during his visit to [St. Louis](/wiki/St._Louis,_Missouri "St. Louis, Missouri").[[27]](#cite_note-CCM_21_10-27) [The Insyderz](/wiki/The_Insyderz "The Insyderz") best known for their renditions of ska-styled [hymns](/wiki/Hymns "Hymns") and modern [praise and worship](/wiki/Contemporary_worship_music "Contemporary worship music") songs,[[28]](#cite_note-CCM_22_5-28) of which they released two albums, *[Skalleluia!](/wiki/Skalleluia! "Skalleluia!")* and *[Skalleluia Too!](/wiki/Skalleluia_Too! "Skalleluia Too!")*.[[9]](#cite_note-JJT_RBW_2000-9) [Five Iron Frenzy](/wiki/Five_Iron_Frenzy "Five Iron Frenzy") became known for their positive but serious lyrics and slapstick satirical humour.[[29]](#cite_note-tRG_3-8-29) Common themes included social causes such as [hypocrisy](/wiki/Hypocrisy "Hypocrisy"), racism, [big business](/wiki/Big_business "Big business"), as well as the renewal of faith.[[29]](#cite_note-tRG_3-8-29)

Ska bands received a small amount of attention from Christian music award shows such as the [Doves](/wiki/Dove_Awards "Dove Awards"). In 1998 the Supertones's [debut album](/wiki/Adventures_of_the_O.C._Supertones "Adventures of the O.C. Supertones") was nominated in the "Alternate / Modern Rock" category, a classification which they denied.[[7]](#cite_note-TTN_W_1998-7) The same year the video for Five Iron's "[A Flowery Song](/wiki/Upbeats_and_Beatdowns "Upbeats and Beatdowns")" was nominated in the "short form video" category.[[30]](#cite_note-BB_110_11-30) At the 1999 awards, The Insyderz cover of [Rich Mullins](/wiki/Rich_Mullins "Rich Mullins")' song "[Awesome God](/wiki/Awesome_God "Awesome God")" won in the "Hard Music Song" category.[[8]](#cite_note-Powell_Fe_ins-8) [The W's](/wiki/The_W%27s "The W's") won two Doves at the 1999 awards, for [their debut](/wiki/Fourth_from_the_Last "Fourth from the Last") as "Modern Rock Album," and "Modern Rock Song" for the song "The Devil Is Bad".[[31]](#cite_note-Powell_Fe_thews-31) They played a [neo-swing](/wiki/Neo-swing "Neo-swing") sound with heavy ska influences. Other career highlights opening for Christian rock heavyweight [dc Talk](/wiki/Dc_Talk "Dc Talk"), and for the Pope during the same visit as the Supertones (although not at the main event).[[27]](#cite_note-CCM_21_10-27) Christian swing music is generally congruent with the [swing revival](/wiki/Swing_revival "Swing revival") of the 1990s,[[32]](#cite_note-7b_21-32) and includes a [small number of bands](/w/index.php?title=List_of_Christian_instrumental/jazz/swing_artists&action=edit&redlink=1 "List of Christian instrumental/jazz/swing artists (page does not exist)"). Of all the Christian bands which took part of the swing revival movement they were the best known.[[31]](#cite_note-Powell_Fe_thews-31)

The sound became most widespread and visible within Christian music from 1997[[1]](#cite_note-CCM_20_1_CS-1) until the early 2000s. During this period Christian ska bands regularly appeared on [Billboard charts](/wiki/Billboard_charts "Billboard charts").[[33]](#cite_note-7b_18-33) The *Encyclopedia of Contemporary Christian Music* characterizes ska as being "one of the more crowded genres in the Christian market" by the turn of the millennium.[[21]](#cite_note-Powell_Fe_fif-21) While producing *Skanktified*, a compilation album released by Eclectica Music (an imprint of [N-Soul](/w/index.php?title=N-Soul_Records&action=edit&redlink=1 "N-Soul Records (page does not exist)")) in 1998, Mark Morrison found over seventy Christian ska bands operating throughout the United States, mostly in [underground scenes](/wiki/Underground_music "Underground music").[[33]](#cite_note-7b_18-33) There were in fact an exceptional number of Christian ska bands underground,[[9]](#cite_note-JJT_RBW_2000-9)[[21]](#cite_note-Powell_Fe_fif-21)[[34]](#cite_note-7b_26-34) creating an oversaturated market for the sound.[[9]](#cite_note-JJT_RBW_2000-9)[[34]](#cite_note-7b_26-34)[[35]](#cite_note-CM_47-35) For instance one band, the Skadaddles, released three albums in 1999, with a sound evolving from ska toward punk and emo.[[36]](#cite_note-Powell_Fe_theskd-36)

A number of other bands released two or more albums to the national scene. Freeto Boat released one ska album in 1998 and moved to a punk sound for their sophomore release in 2000. In a move to distance themselves from ska the band changed their name twice and picked up an emo-indie-punk sound before signing to a [major label](/wiki/Tooth_%26_Nail_Records "Tooth & Nail Records").[[37]](#cite_note-Pow_FB-37)[[38]](#cite_note-FJacks_CWM-38) Flight One Eighty released three albums - one of ska, one of swing, and finally a [ska-punk](/wiki/Ska-punk "Ska-punk")/[power pop](/wiki/Power_pop "Power pop") release.[[39]](#cite_note-Powell_Fe_F180-39) [The Dingees](/wiki/The_Dingees "The Dingees") were a spinoff band of the Supertones who play a sound which mixes punk, ska, and reggae, and have released three albums, [the final one](/wiki/The_Crucial_Conspiracy "The Crucial Conspiracy") in 2001.[[40]](#cite_note-Powell_Fe_theding-40) BUCK Enterprises shows an evolution in sound more like that of the Supertones, changing their sound mix from having a dominating horn section on their [national debut](/w/index.php?title=BUCK&action=edit&redlink=1 "BUCK (page does not exist)") (1998)[[41]](#cite_note-Powell_Fe_BUCK-41) to one which "flirts with ska" by 2001.[[42]](#cite_note-CCM_24_1-42)[[43]](#cite_note-HM_01_90-43)

The big three bands of Christian ska each continued until the early to mid 2000s. Five Iron continued to draw large crowds; a tour in 2001 drew an audience of 1,200 per night.[[44]](#cite_note-CCM_24_7_SP-44) They also participated in the [Vans Warped Tour](/wiki/Vans_Warped_Tour "Vans Warped Tour") in 2002 before breaking up the following year. Their final show had an attendance of over 3,500.[[45]](#cite_note-Argyrakis_Ivew-45) After a period of relative inactivity, The Insyderz released [their final album](/wiki/Soundtrack_to_a_Revolution "Soundtrack to a Revolution") in 2003 and continued to tour until 2005. That same year also saw the end of the Supertones, who had released [their final studio album](/wiki/Revenge_of_the_O.C._Supertones "Revenge of the O.C. Supertones") one year prior.

### Reunions (late 2000s through present)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Christian_ska&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Reunions (late 2000s through present)")]

In 2009, The Insyderz announced they were reuniting. In 2011, they began a Kickstarter campaign to raise funds for the first album since breaking up, *The Sinner's Songbook*, which succeeded and the album was released. In 2010, The O.C. Supertones announced they were reuniting for the summer to play some festivals, and have since remained together. The album *For the Glory* was released. Also in 2010, The Dingees released their fourth album, *The Rebel Soul Sound System*, a heavily reggae-influenced album.

On November 22, 2011, Five Iron Frenzy announced they were reuniting and released a new single, "It Was a Dark and Stormy Night". They began a Kickstarter project that night to fund their new album. The $30,000 goal was reached in less than an hour. It went on to raise $207,980 USD, making it the most funded Kickstarter by dollar amount ever on the day it ended (January 21, 2012). Their new album, Engine of a Million Plots, was released on November 25, 2013.[[46]](#cite_note-46)

Also in 2011, BUCK Enterprises played a one-night-only reunion show on December 30, 2011 in Wixom, Michigan. Squad Five-O reunited to play at [Cornerstone 2012](/wiki/Cornerstone_Festival "Cornerstone Festival").
